items.subtopic: Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff
items.title: Understanding the Differential Diagnosis of Dizziness: Vestibular vs. Presyncopal Causes
items.content: Distinguishing between vestibular and presyncopal causes of dizziness is critical for appropriate management. Vestibular dizziness includes vertigo or imbalance due to peripheral disorders affecting labyrinths, vestibular nerves, or central pathways. Acute unilateral lesions cause vertigo from sudden vestibular input imbalances, while bilateral lesions lead to oscillopsia and instability during head movements. Presyncopal dizziness results from brain hypoperfusion due to cardiac dysrhythmias, orthostatic hypotension, medication effects, or other causes, potentially leading to syncope if not corrected.
keyPoints: Vestibular dizziness can be peripheral (affecting labyrinths/nerves) or central (disruption of pathways).
Acute unilateral vestibular lesions cause vertigo; bilateral lesions lead to oscillopsia and instability.
Presyncopal dizziness results from brain hypoperfusion due to cardiac dysrhythmias, orthostatic hypotension, medication effects, or other causes.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff

items.subtopic: Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff
items.title: Evaluating Dizziness: Key Questions and Considerations
items.content: When evaluating dizziness, it is essential to determine if the condition poses immediate danger (e.g., arrhythmia or transient ischemic attack/stroke), whether it is vestibular in nature, and if so, whether it originates from peripheral or central causes. A detailed history and examination are often sufficient to guide further studies or referrals.
keyPoints: Key evaluation questions include: Is the dizziness dangerous? Is it vestibular? If vestibular, is it peripheral or central?
A careful history and physical examination can often determine the need for additional studies or specialist referral.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff

items.subtopic: HISTORY | EXAMINATION
items.title: Characterizing Dizziness: Timing and Associated Symptoms
items.content: Distinguishing between peripheral and central causes of dizziness involves evaluating the timing of episodes and associated symptoms. Brief episodes (seconds) often indicate benign paroxysmal positional vertigo or orthostatic hypotension, while longer episodes (hours) suggest vestibular migraine or Ménière's disease. Intermediate durations (minutes) raise suspicion for transient ischemic attacks. Accompanying symptoms like unilateral hearing loss and acute aural symptoms point to peripheral causes, whereas double vision, numbness, and limb ataxia suggest central lesions.
keyPoints: Brief dizziness episodes (seconds): BPPV, orthostatic hypotension
Longer episodes (hours): vestibular migraine, Ménière's disease
Intermediate duration (minutes): consider transient ischemic attacks
Unilateral hearing loss and acute aural symptoms: peripheral cause
Double vision, numbness, limb ataxia: central lesion
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · HISTORY | EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: HISTORY | EXAMINATION
items.title: Ocular Motility Examination in Dizziness Evaluation
items.content: Assessing ocular motility is crucial for differentiating between peripheral and central causes of dizziness. Disconjugate eye movements suggest peripheral disorders like cranial neuropathies or muscle weakness. Poor pursuit or dysmetric saccades indicate central pathology, often involving the cerebellum. A vertical misalignment in a cover test may point to brainstem or cerebellar lesions. Spontaneous nystagmus, particularly of the jerk type, is common except in acute vestibulopathy.
keyPoints: Disconjugate eye movements: peripheral disorders
Poor pursuit/dysmetric saccades: central pathology (cerebellum)
Vertical misalignment in cover test: brainstem/cerebellar lesion
Spontaneous nystagmus (jerk type): common except in acute vestibulopathy
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · HISTORY | EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 24-1 Features of Peripheral and Central Vertigo
items.title: Distinguishing Peripheral from Central Vertigo via Nystagmus Characteristics
items.content: Nystagmus characteristics can help differentiate between peripheral and central vertigo. Unidirectional nystagmus with fast phases beating away from the ear with the lesion suggests a peripheral cause, while direction-changing nystagmus indicates a central origin.
keyPoints: Unidirectional nystagmus in peripheral lesions beats away from the affected ear.
Nystagmus that changes direction with gaze is indicative of a central lesion.
Pure vertical or pure torsional nystagmus suggests a central cause, unlike transient mixed vertical-torsional nystagmus seen in BPPV.
Peripheral nystagmus may be inhibited by visual fixation; central nystagmus is not.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · TABLE 24-1 Features of Peripheral and Central Vertigo

items.subtopic: TABLE 24-1 Features of Peripheral and Central Vertigo
items.title: Utilizing the Head Impulse Test to Assess Vestibular Function
items.content: The head impulse test evaluates peripheral vestibular function by assessing the vestibuloocular reflex (VOR) with rapid head rotations. A deficient VOR is indicated by catch-up saccades in the opposite direction of head rotation.
keyPoints: Head impulse test involves small-amplitude (~20 degrees) rapid head rotations while fixating on a target.
Catch-up saccade after head rotation indicates vestibular hypofunction.
Unilateral dysfunction is suggested by catch-up saccades during rotation toward the weak side.
Bilateral dysfunction is indicated by catch-up saccades in both directions.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · TABLE 24-1 Features of Peripheral and Central Vertigo

items.subtopic: ANCILLARY TESTING | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT | ACUTE PROLONGED VERTIGO (VESTIBULAR NEURITIS)
items.title: When to Order MRI in Unexplained Vestibular Symptoms
items.content: MRI of the internal auditory canals with gadolinium is recommended for patients presenting with unexplained unilateral hearing loss or vestibular hypofunction. This imaging helps rule out serious conditions such as a vestibular schwannoma, which could be causing these symptoms.
keyPoints: MRI with gadolinium should be performed in cases of unexplained unilateral hearing loss or vestibular hypofunction to exclude schwannomas.
Audiometry is essential for suspected vestibular disorders and can indicate peripheral issues like vestibular schwannoma if there's unilateral sensorineural hearing loss.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · ANCILLARY TESTING | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT | ACUTE PROLONGED VERTIGO (VESTIBULAR NEURITIS)

items.subtopic: ANCILLARY TESTING | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT | ACUTE PROLONGED VERTIGO (VESTIBULAR NEURITIS)
items.title: Management of Vestibular Neuritis
items.content: In vestibular neuritis, spontaneous recovery is common. However, early use of glucocorticoids remains uncertain due to conflicting study results. Antiviral therapy is not recommended unless herpes zoster oticus is suspected. Vestibular suppressants should be avoided after the initial days to prevent hindering central compensation and recovery.
keyPoints: Most patients with vestibular neuritis recover spontaneously, but some may experience chronic dizziness or motion sensitivity.
The role of early glucocorticoid therapy in vestibular neuritis is uncertain; studies have shown mixed results.
Antiviral medications are not typically used unless herpes zoster oticus (Ramsay Hunt syndrome) is suspected.
Vestibular suppressants should be avoided after the first few days to allow central compensation and recovery.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · ANCILLARY TESTING | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT | ACUTE PROLONGED VERTIGO (VESTIBULAR NEURITIS)

items.subtopic: BENIGN PAROXYSMAL POSITIONAL VERTIGO | VESTIBULAR MIGRAINE
items.title: Diagnosing BPPV: Recognizing Characteristic Features and Treatment
items.content: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV) is identified by brief episodes of vertigo (<1 min, typically 15–20 s) triggered by head position changes. It is caused by dislodged otoconia in the semicircular canals, most commonly the posterior canal, leading to characteristic nystagmus. Diagnosis is supported by specific maneuvers like rolling over in bed, and treatment involves repositioning maneuvers such as the Epley maneuver.
keyPoints: Episodes of vertigo last <1 min, typically 15–20 s
Triggered by head position changes relative to gravity
Caused by dislodged otoconia in semicircular canals (usually posterior)
Characteristic nystagmus: upward and torsional for posterior canal BPPV
Treated with repositioning maneuvers like the Epley maneuver
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · BENIGN PAROXYSMAL POSITIONAL VERTIGO | VESTIBULAR MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: BENIGN PAROXYSMAL POSITIONAL VERTIGO | VESTIBULAR MIGRAINE
items.title: Understanding Vestibular Migraine: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Vestibular migraine is a frequent cause of episodic vertigo, often associated with or preceding migraine headaches. Vertigo can last from minutes to hours, sometimes without headache or with only mild symptoms. Patients may experience motion sensitivity and other migraine features like photophobia or phonophobia. Diagnosis relies on clinical history, and treatment typically involves migraine prophylaxis medications.
keyPoints: Vertigo duration: minutes to hours
May precede or occur without a typical migraine headache
Common symptoms include motion sensitivity and visual motion sensitivity
Other migraine features may be present (photophobia, phonophobia)
Treated with prophylactic medications for migraines
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · BENIGN PAROXYSMAL POSITIONAL VERTIGO | VESTIBULAR MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: MÉNIÈRE’S DISEASE | VESTIBULAR SCHWANNOMA | BILATERAL VESTIBULAR HYPOFUNCTION
items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Ménière's Disease
items.content: Ménière's disease is characterized by vertigo, hearing loss, and aural fullness. It is distinguished from other vestibular disorders by asymmetric low-frequency hearing loss during attacks. Audiometry confirms this pattern, with potential improvement between episodes. The pathophysiology involves endolymphatic hydrops, although the exact mechanism remains unclear. Initial management includes diuretics and sodium restriction.
keyPoints: Key features: vertigo, hearing loss, aural fullness
Audiometry shows asymmetric low-frequency hearing loss during attacks
Initial treatments: diuretics and sodium restriction
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · MÉNIÈRE’S DISEASE | VESTIBULAR SCHWANNOMA | BILATERAL VESTIBULAR HYPOFUNCTION

items.subtopic: MÉNIÈRE’S DISEASE | VESTIBULAR SCHWANNOMA | BILATERAL VESTIBULAR HYPOFUNCTION
items.title: Diagnosis of Vestibular Schwannoma in Unilateral Sensorineural Hearing Loss
items.content: Vestibular schwannomas cause progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss and vestibular hypofunction without vertigo due to central compensation. Diagnosis is often delayed until significant hearing loss occurs. The head impulse test shows deficient response on the affected side, but nystagmus is not prominent. MRI of the internal auditory canals is essential for diagnosis in patients with unexplained unilateral sensorineural hearing loss or vestibular hypofunction.
keyPoints: Symptoms: progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, no vertigo
Head impulse test shows deficient response on affected side
Diagnostic imaging: MRI of internal auditory canals
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · MÉNIÈRE’S DISEASE | VESTIBULAR SCHWANNOMA | BILATERAL VESTIBULAR HYPOFUNCTION

items.subtopic: CENTRAL VESTIBULAR DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL DIZZINESS | TREATMENT
items.title: Acute Central Vertigo: A Medical Emergency
items.content: Central lesions causing vertigo, involving brainstem and/or cerebellum pathways, can be life-threatening due to potential stroke or hemorrhage. Immediate MRI and neurologic evaluation are crucial.
keyPoints: Acute central vertigo is a medical emergency.
Potential causes include ischemic/hemorrhagic stroke, demyelination, tumors, or neurodegenerative conditions.
All patients with suspected central vestibular disorders should undergo brain MRI and full neurologic evaluation.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · CENTRAL VESTIBULAR DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL DIZZINESS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: CENTRAL VESTIBULAR DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL DIZZINESS | TREATMENT
items.title: Management of Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness (PPPD)
items.content: PPPD involves chronic dizziness and disequilibrium, worsened by motion. Treatment includes SSRIs, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and vestibular rehabilitation, while avoiding vestibular suppressants.
keyPoints: PPPD is characterized by a chronic feeling of fluctuating dizziness lasting 3 months or longer.
Symptoms are worse with self-motion and visual motion, especially in complex environments.
Treatment includes SSRIs, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and vestibular rehabilitation; avoid vestibular suppressants.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · CENTRAL VESTIBULAR DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL DIZZINESS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff
items.question: Which of the following best describes vertigo as mentioned in the passage?
options: A sense of spinning or other motion that may be physiological or pathological.
A presyncopal sensation resulting from brain hypoperfusion.
A feeling of imbalance due to proprioceptive loss.
An anxiety-related symptom without a clear physical cause.
items.explanation: Vertigo is described as a sense of spinning or motion that can be physiological (e.g., after head rotation) or pathological (due to vestibular dysfunction).

items.subtopic: Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff
items.question: What is the primary cause of presyncopal dizziness according to the passage?
options: Brain hypoperfusion due to cardiac dysrhythmia or orthostatic hypotension.
Loss of proprioception from sensory neuropathy.
items.explanation: Presyncopal dizziness is primarily caused by brain hypoperfusion, which can result from cardiac dysrhythmia or orthostatic hypotension.
items.reference: Dizziness and Vertigo · Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff

items.subtopic: Mark F. Walker, Robert B. Daroff
items.question: According to the passage, what type of lesions cause vertigo due to a sudden imbalance in vestibular inputs?
items.explanation: Acute unilateral lesions cause vertigo due to a sudden imbalance in vestibular inputs from the two labyrinths.
